perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement will begin on the date of this Agreement and will end on October 3, 2005 unless terminated earlier by one party upon giving the other party at least 30 days written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement will begin on the date of this Agreement and will end on October 3, 2005 unless terminated earlier by one party upon giving the other party at least 14 days written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 14 days may conflict with reasonable notice requirements under contract law, particularly if the agreement involves significant ongoing obligations or investments. In British Columbia, what constitutes reasonable notice can depend on the specific context and may exceed 14 days.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Potentially conflicts with common law principles of reasonable notice for contract termination in British Columbia.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles of Contract Law in British Columbia
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, common law requires reasonable notice for contract termination. 14 days may be deemed unreasonable depending on the nature of the contract and the reliance of the parties. The first URL links to a court case discussing reasonable notice and the second provides a summary of the principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Commercial Transport Act Search Results | Clear Search | Previous (in doc) | Next (in doc) | Prev Doc | Next Doc Copyright © King's Printer, Victoria, British Columbia, Canada Licence Disclaimer This Act is current to July 8, 2025 See the Tables of Legislative Changes for this Act’s legislative history, including any changes not in force. Commercial Transport Act [RSBC 1996] CHAPTER 58 Contents 1 Definitions 2 Minister's responsibilities 3 Act not to apply to some road building machines 4 Motor Vehicle Act provisions 5 Motor vehicle regulations 6 Registration, licence and insurance 7 Motor Fuel Tax Act 8 Overload, crossing and oversize permits 9 Government, municipal and school vehicles exempt 10 Prorating of fees and reciprocity of agreements with other governments 11 Power to make regulations 12 Offences and penalties 13 Arrest 14 Disposal of taxes and fines Definitions 1 In this Act: "axle" means an axle as defined in the regulations; "axle unit" means an axle unit as defined in the regulations; "combination of vehicles" means every combination of truck, truck tractor, semi-trailer and trailer; "commercial vehicle" includes (a) a motor vehicle having permanently attached to it a truck or delivery body, (b) an ambulance, casket wagon, fire apparatus, hearse, motor bus, tow car, road building machine and tractor, (b.1) a taxi, other than a taxi in a prescribed class of taxi, (c) a combination of vehicles, and (d) other vehicles as specified by regulation of the Lieutenant Governor in Council; "gross vehicle weight" means the number of kilograms derived by adding the weights on all the axles of a commercial vehicle; "group of axles" means a group of axles as defined in the regulations; "inspector" means a person designated under the Motor Vehicle Act as a commercial transport inspector; "licensed gross vehicle weight" means the gross vehicle weight for which a commercial vehicle is licensed; "minister" includes any person designated in writing by the minister; "motor vehicle" means a vehicle designed to be self-propelled, but does not include (a) a vehicle operating wholly on fixed rails or tracks, (b) an electric trolley bus, or (c) a motor assisted cycle within the meaning of the Motor Vehicle Act ; "motor vehicle liability policy" means a certificate issued under Part 1 of the Insurance (Vehicle) Act and the regulations under that Part; "overload" means the larger of the following: (a) the number of kilograms derived by subtracting from the gross vehicle weight of a commercial vehicle the licensed gross vehicle weight; (b) the number of kilograms derived by subtracting from the weight on any one axle or combination of axles of a commercial vehicle the weight authorized by regulation to be carried on the axle or combination of axles; "oversize" means the amount derived by subtracting from the outside width, height or overall length of a commercial vehicle with its load, if any, the permissible outside width, height or overall length prescribed under this Act; "road building machine" means a vehicle (a) that is designed and used primarily for grading of highways, paving of highways, earth moving and other construction work on highways, (b) that is not designed or used primarily for the transportation otherwise of persons or property, and (c) that is only incidentally operated or moved over a highway, and includes a vehicle designated as a road building machine by order of the Lieutenant Governor in Council, but does not include (d) a vehicle originally designed for the transportation of persons, or property to which machinery has been attached, or (e) dump trucks originally designed to comply with the size and weight provisions of the regulations; "semi-trailer" includes (a) a vehicle without motive power designed to be drawn by a motor vehicle or truck tractor and so constructed that an appreciable part of its weight and that of its load rests on and is carried by the motor vehicle or truck tractor, and (b) a pole trailer bu
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 30 days written notice for termination, whereas the changed contract reduces this to 14 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest this could be deemed unreasonable, potentially conflicting with common law requirements for reasonable notice, depending on the specific context and reliance of the parties. While the provided law snippet focuses on the Commercial Transport Act and doesn't directly address contract termination notice periods, the core issue is whether the reduced notice period is reasonable under general contract law principles. The change weakens a previously defined obligation, opening it up to legal challenge under the principle of 'reasonable notice,' thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will use best efforts to maintain the confidentiality of the Information both during and after the term of this Agreement and will not disclose the Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the University for a period of three years from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will maintain the confidentiality of the Information as long as they deem necessary and will not disclose the Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the University for a period of one year from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year could be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the laws of British Columbia, particularly if the information has a longer commercial life or requires more than one year to reverse engineer. This could violate the implied duty to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Potential conflict with trade secret protection under common law and implied duties of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles of Trade Secret Protection in Canada
perturbation.law_explanation: While Canada does not have specific trade secrets legislation, the common law protects trade secrets through breach of confidence. One year may be deemed inadequate to protect the University's confidential information, especially if it has a longer commercial life or would take longer than one year to properly protect. The first URL provides an overview and the second, a discussion of trade secret law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under British Columbia law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides an interpretation act, it doesn't directly address the adequacy of a one-year confidentiality period for trade secrets. However, the Law Explanation states that one year might be inadequate to protect the University's confidential information, especially if it has a longer commercial life. The original text provided a longer period of protection that the modified text changed, which could violate the implied duty to protect trade secrets, as explained. Given the potential inadequacy of the reduced time frame, the modified contract arguably contradicts the need to protect trade secrets for a reasonable amount of time.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed under the laws of British Columbia and the applicable laws of Canada without reference to its conflict of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed under the laws of Yukon and the applicable laws of Canada without reference to its conflict of law rules.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Yukon, while still within Canada, could create enforceability issues if the University of British Columbia does not have any connection to Yukon. The agreement was negotiated and is being performed in British Columbia, so changing the jurisdiction to Yukon could conflict with established principles of private international law, specifically concerning choice of law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Private International Law (Choice of Law)
perturbation.law_citation: Choice of Law Principles in Canadian Private International Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Choice of law requires a real and substantial connection to the chosen jurisdiction. Yukon has no connection to the parties or transaction. The first URL is the Access to Justice Act of Ontario, containing provisions relating to choice of law and the second URL discusses choice of law principles.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from British Columbia to Yukon. The Perturbation Explanation states this could create enforceability issues if the University of British Columbia does not have any connection to Yukon, as the agreement was negotiated and is being performed in British Columbia. The Law Explanation also emphasizes that choice of law requires a real and substantial connection to the chosen jurisdiction, and Yukon has no connection to the parties or transaction. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 404 error, the combined explanations indicate that choosing Yukon law could contradict principles requiring a reasonable connection to the chosen jurisdiction, as that connection is absent. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition because the change introduces a requirement (Yukon law) that could violate established legal principles regarding choice of law.

perturbation.original_text: At the written request of the University or upon expiry or earlier termination of this Agreement, Recipient will, on the direction of the University, return or destroy the Information and will not retain any photocopy or other reproduction of any part of the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: At the written request of the University, the Recipient will destroy the Information and will not retain any photocopy or other reproduction of any part of the Information. Upon expiry or earlier termination of this Agreement, the Recipient is not obligated to destroy or return the Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to destroy or return confidential information upon expiry or termination undermines the entire purpose of the non-disclosure agreement. By stating the Recipient is *not* obligated to destroy or return the Information, the modification significantly weakens the protection of the University's intellectual property after the agreement ends. This would contradict the principles of the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian contract law includes an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Allowing the Recipient to retain and use the confidential information indefinitely after termination arguably violates this duty. The first URL links to the SCC decision establishing the duty of honest contractual performance and the second is a discussion of the case from a legal firm.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon the expiry or termination of the agreement, which fundamentally undermines the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement. This directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in Canadian contract law, as the recipient could potentially retain and use confidential information indefinitely, violating the University's intellectual property rights. The law explanation points out that allowing the Recipient to retain and use the confidential information indefinitely after termination arguably violates this duty. The changed text creates an inconsistency related to the protection of intellectual property that the agreement was originally purposed to protect.
